# Acts 2: The Gift Given and the Response Demanded

What happens on Pentecost is not primarily a display of power for wonder's sake. The wind, the fire, the ability to speak languages no one had learned—these are signs that God Himself has entered the moment with dramatic force. But notice what they announce: not spiritual ecstasy or private encounters, but the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ and the call to repentance. When the Spirit fills the believers, they immediately testify to what God has done in history, to concrete events that happened to a particular Man in Jerusalem. The crowd that gathers is bewildered not because they witness something mystical, but because ordinary Galileans are communicating to them in their own native languages about God's mighty acts. The miracle serves the message. Every tongue declared the same gospel truth: Jesus of Nazareth, crucified and raised, is Lord and Christ.

Peter's sermon cuts through the confusion with relentless clarity. He doesn't apologize for the disciples' behavior or try to make Christianity palatable. Instead, he looks the crowd in the eye and says: "You crucified Him." He quotes Scripture to show that what happened—that death could not hold the Holy One, that He ascended to God's right hand—was prophesied centuries before. And he makes the implications inescapable: the One you killed, God has made both Lord and Christ. This is not a suggestion for spiritual seekers. It is a declaration of what is true about reality itself. When the people hear this, they are "cut to the heart"—and their response is the only one that makes sense: "What shall we do?"

Peter's answer carries both urgency and grace. Repent. Be baptized in Jesus' name. You will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit—the same Spirit who empowered the disciples, now offered to them. The promise extends beyond that moment: to their children, to the distant nations, to all whom the Lord calls. Three thousand respond and are baptized that day. But what follows matters equally—they do not scatter or return to their former lives. Instead, they devote themselves to apostolic teaching, to shared life, to prayer, to breaking bread together. They sell possessions to meet real needs. They gather daily. They share meals with genuine joy. They praise God while gaining the favor of those around them, and the Lord continually adds to their number those being saved.

This is what the gift of the Holy Spirit actually produces: not isolated spiritual experiences, but a community radically reoriented toward Christ and toward one another. The Spirit doesn't make you feel good in private. The Spirit breaks you open to hear that you have killed the Son of God, pierces you with that knowledge, and then transforms you so completely that you no longer hoard what you have but share it freely, meeting the needs of others. The young church's devotion is not to an idea or a feeling but to apostolic teaching—to the reliable word about Jesus—and their unity flows from that common allegiance. If you have believed in Jesus Christ, the same Spirit dwells in you. The question that faced those three thousand at Pentecost faces you now: What will this gift demand of your life?
